citation: Baker HP, Volchenko E, Rossi D, Dayton SR, Tjong VK. Analysis of National Football League preseason injuries resulting in roster cuts: a retrospective review. Curr Orthop Pract. 2021;32(1):1-5.
abstract: This retrospective review examined all injuries sustained by NFL athletes during the 2014–2019 preseasons that resulted in a player being cut from the team roster. A total of 110 injuries were identified from publicly available injury reports across 32 NFL teams. The incidence of a preseason injury leading to a roster cut was 1.59 per 1,000 athletic exposures (AEs). The lower extremity was the most frequently injured body region (45% of injuries), and undrafted free agents comprised the majority of cut players (63%). Most athletes (75%) did not play another year in the NFL following a preseason roster cut.
tldr: A preseason injury results in a roster cut at a rate of 1.59 per 1,000 AEs, and 75% of those players never play another NFL season. Being undrafted or drafted in round 7 is the dominant risk profile — over 75% of cut players fell into these two groups.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports from the 2014–2019 preseasons; injury incidence calculated per 1,000 AEs (one AE = one athlete appearing in one game); demographic data including draft round, years in NFL, position, body area, and pathology collected from team websites and public sources; SPSS 25 used for descriptive statistics.
findings:
• 110 injuries identified across 2014–2019 NFL preseasons resulted in a roster cut
• Injury incidence prior to cut: 1.59 per 1,000 AEs
• Most common position cut: defensive back (24 players, 22%), followed by linebacker (19, 17%) and tight end (16, 15%)
• 69 players (63%) were undrafted; 14 (13%) were 7th-round picks
• Rookies comprised 35% of cut players; 75% had 0 years of prior NFL experience
• Lower extremity most common body area (45%); ankle sprains most common pathology (14 injuries, 13%), followed by knee (12, 11%)
• 82 of 110 players (75%) did not play another NFL season after being cut
• Only 28 players (25%) made another 53-man roster; only 3 cases (3%) required surgery
limitations: Retrospective design relying on publicly available injury data without access to team physician records or NFL Injury Surveillance System (NFLISS); specific pathology details and surgical information often unavailable; unable to determine causal factors (player talent, team finances) for cut decisions; concussion data likely underreported; surgery data may be substantially underreported.
practicalImplications: Preseason injury risk is concentrated in rookie and undrafted players, suggesting these athletes may benefit from enhanced preseason injury surveillance and load management protocols. NFL teams should weigh the financial disincentive to retain injured borderline-roster players against long-term development value. Defensive backs and linebackers are at highest positional risk for preseason roster cuts following injury.
athleteDev: In American football, undrafted and late-round players face career-ending consequences from preseason injuries at disproportionate rates. Programs should identify these high-risk athletes early and implement position-specific lower extremity injury prevention programs. The data support prioritizing ankle and knee injury prevention — particularly for defensive backs — during preseason camps.
rtp: 75% of NFL players who sustained a preseason injury that led to a roster cut never played another NFL season, underscoring the career-ending potential of preseason injuries for marginal roster players. Only 25% made another 53-man roster. RTP decisions for preseason injuries must account not only for clinical recovery but also roster and contract implications; early physician referral and transparent communication with player personnel staff is essential.

citation: Altarriba-Bartes A, Drobnic F, Til L, Malliaropoulos N, Montoro JB, Cos F. Analysis of Recovery Methods' Efficacy Applied up to 72 Hours Postmatch in Professional Football: A Systematic Review With Graded Recommendations. J Strength Cond Res. 2020;34(3):829-848.
abstract: This systematic review evaluated the efficacy of recovery methods applied within 72 hours of competitive match play in professional football (soccer). Thirty studies meeting inclusion criteria were analyzed, covering modalities including cold-water immersion, contrast water therapy, active recovery, compression garments, sleep, nutrition, and massage. Recovery outcomes assessed included muscle damage biomarkers, perceived muscle soreness, neuromuscular function, and perceptual wellness. Graded recommendations were assigned to each modality based on the level and quality of evidence.
tldr: No single recovery modality has strong Level A evidence in professional football post-match. Cold-water immersion and sleep/nutrition carry the best current evidence, while most other modalities receive only C-grade recommendations due to low-quality or inconsistent data. Practitioners should prioritize sleep and nutrition as the foundation of any post-match recovery protocol.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA guidelines; electronic database search (PubMed, SPORTDiscus, CINAHL, Cochrane) through 2018; 30 studies included after screening; recovery modalities categorized and evidence graded using a modified Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine framework (Level A–D); outcomes included CK, LDH, muscle soreness (VAS/DOMS), jump performance, sprint performance, and perceived recovery.
findings:
• Cold-water immersion (CWI): moderate evidence for reducing perceived muscle soreness and CK; best supported post-match modality
• Contrast water therapy: some evidence for perceptual recovery but limited objective performance data
• Active recovery: inconsistent evidence; may benefit low-intensity clearance sessions
• Compression garments: limited evidence in football-specific populations
• Sleep and nutrition: foundational to recovery; indirect but strong mechanistic support
• Massage: limited and low-quality evidence in professional football context
• No modality achieved Level A evidence in professional football-specific populations
• Most interventions studied at ≤24h post-match; 48–72h window is understudied
• Overall evidence base is limited by heterogeneous study designs, small samples, and lack of ecological validity
limitations: High heterogeneity in study designs, populations, intervention protocols, and outcome measures; most studies conducted on amateur or semi-professional players; ecological validity of laboratory-based recovery studies to actual match-play scenarios is limited; publication bias likely; recovery outcomes often assessed at a single post-match time point rather than the full 72-hour window; competition schedule and prior training load not consistently controlled.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should implement a tiered post-match recovery protocol with sleep and nutrition as non-negotiable foundations, followed by CWI (10–15 min at 10–15°C) as the most evidence-supported active modality. Decisions about additional modalities (contrast water therapy, compression, massage) should be guided by individual player response, logistical feasibility, and match schedule. Research gaps at 48–72 hours post-match are significant and limit confident prescription for congested fixture periods.
athleteDev: Note: This paper examines professional football (soccer), not American football. The post-match recovery modalities reviewed — CWI, contrast therapy, active recovery, nutrition, sleep — are broadly applicable to American football, particularly in the 24–72 hours following games. The lack of Level A evidence for any single modality supports the use of individualized, multi-modal protocols rather than prescriptive team-wide approaches.
rtp: The 72-hour post-match recovery window is critical for neuromuscular restoration before the next training session. CWI remains the most evidence-supported active recovery tool, with the strongest effects on perceived soreness and inflammatory markers. For RTP planning, the understudied 48–72h window is particularly relevant in congested schedules; practitioners should monitor neuromuscular readiness (e.g., CMJ force-time metrics) alongside perceptual data throughout this window.

citation: Novak AR, Impellizzeri FM, Trivedi A, Coutts AJ, McCall A. Analysis of the worst-case scenarios in an elite football team: Towards a better understanding and application. J Sports Sci. 2021;39(20):2323-2334.
abstract: This study investigated variability in the worst-case scenario (WCS) and proposed a framework to improve its definition and guide further investigation in professional football (soccer). Optical tracking data from 26 male players across 38 English Premier League matches were analyzed for total distance, high-speed running (>5.5 m/s), and sprinting (>7.0 m/s) using a 3-minute rolling window. Linear mixed effects models examined contextual factors hypothesized to associate with WCS values, including player position, match half, time of occurrence, starter vs. substitute status, and prior epoch load. Unexplained variance remained high, with intra-individual CVs ranging from 4.6–76.4% across metrics.
tldr: Note: This paper studies soccer (English Premier League). The WCS as defined by rolling time windows is highly variable and context-dependent, making its use as a training benchmark conceptually questionable. Coaching and sport science staff should treat WCS as a complex composite construct rather than a simple threshold to replicate in training.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional study; optical tracking data (TRACAB Gen4, 25 FPS) from 26 male EPL players (age 28±4 y) across 38 matches of the 2018/19 season; WCS defined as peak 3-minute total distance (WCS-TD), high-speed running >5.5 m/s (WCS-HSR), and sprinting >7.0 m/s (WCS-SP) via rolling window; linear mixed effects models with position, match half, time-in-half, starter/substitute, uncoupled total output, pre-epoch measures, and minutes played as fixed effects; player ID and game ID as random effects; AIC model comparison; marginal and conditional R² reported.
findings:
• High intra-individual variability: CV = 4.6–8.2% for WCS-TD, 15.6–37.8% for WCS-HSR, 21.1–76.4% for WCS-SP
• Marginal R² ranged from 0.28–0.40, leaving 47–60% of variance unexplained
• Position affected WCS-TD: Central Midfielders covered the most total distance (mean 500 m); Centre Backs covered the least
• Starters produced higher WCS-TD values than substitutes
• WCS-TD occurred earlier in match half; WCS-SP occurred later
• WCS-HSR associated with fewer minutes played and higher uncoupled total output
• No significant positional differences found for WCS-SP
• WCS for different metrics did not occur concurrently in the same match for individual players
limitations: Only 21 players included after filtering goalkeepers and those with <5 match appearances; data from a single EPL team over one season limits generalizability; analysis restricted to external load metrics (distance, speed) without internal load measures; some players observed <5 times, making individual WCS estimates unreliable; no causal inference possible from cross-sectional design.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should refrain from using the WCS as a simple benchmark for training prescription, as its high variability and multi-factorial nature make it an unstable target. Training drills designed to replicate a single metric's WCS may not expose athletes to the concurrent demands that produce a true WCS. The WCS should be understood as a composite construct requiring both external and internal load context to be meaningful.
athleteDev: Note: This paper is based on soccer (English Premier League). The conceptual critique of WCS applies broadly to any team sport, including American football: peak locomotor demands measured by a single external load metric (e.g., top-speed distance in a 3-minute window) do not capture the full physiological stress an athlete encounters during the most demanding game situations. American football practitioners using GPS/tracking data should interpret rolling-window peak demands with similar caution.
rtp: The concept of targeting an athlete's individual WCS as a return-to-play benchmark is explicitly challenged by this study. Given the high intra-individual variability (CV up to 76% for sprinting WCS), an athlete could theoretically achieve their prior WCS value by chance rather than through genuine readiness. RTP criteria based on WCS should be supplemented with internal load monitoring and contextual match data to avoid clearing athletes prematurely.

citation: Zhao Y, Wei X, Zhao K. Analysis of Z-Score and total score of athleticism on drafted and undrafted players from the NFL scouting combine. Sci Rep. 2025;15:21742.
abstract: This study evaluated the effectiveness of z-scores and Total Score of Athleticism (TSA) in distinguishing drafted from undrafted players in the NFL Scouting Combine (SC). Data from 3,446 players who participated in the NFL SC between 2000 and 2024 were analyzed, grouped into skill players (SP, n=1,318), big skill players (BSP, n=1,067), and linemen (LM, n=1,061). Z-scores were calculated for seven combine tests (40-yard sprint, vertical jump, bench press, broad jump, 3-cone drill, shuttle run) and summed to produce TSA. ROC analysis and binary logistic regression assessed discriminatory accuracy between drafted and undrafted players across positions.
tldr: Drafted NFL players significantly outperform undrafted players on z-scores and TSA across all positions. The 40-yard dash z-score is the single best discriminator (AUC 0.637–0.719), while bench press is the weakest. Position-specific z-score calculations outperform overall-sample calculations for talent identification — coaches should use position-normed combine scores rather than league-wide benchmarks.
methods: Secondary data analysis of NFL SC data (2000–2024) from Pro Football Reference; 3,446 players included after excluding QBs, kickers, punters, long snappers, and players missing tests; grouped into SP (n=1,318), BSP (n=1,067), LM (n=1,061); z-scores calculated as (score − group mean) / SD; TSA = sum of all z-scores; independent t-tests and Mann-Whitney U for drafted vs. undrafted comparisons; ANOVA and Kruskal-Wallis H for positional group comparisons; ROC analysis (AUC) and binary logistic regression; Spearman correlation; SPSS 27 and Origin Pro 2024 used.
findings:
• Drafted players significantly outperformed undrafted players on all z-scores and TSA (p<0.001) across all positions
• 40-yard sprint z-score achieved the highest AUC (0.637–0.719) across all positional groups
• Bench press had the lowest AUC (0.556–0.631), poorest discriminator between drafted/undrafted
• Position-specific z-score and TSA calculations showed better discriminatory accuracy than overall-sample calculations
• SP showed best sprint performance; BSP and LM showed greater upper body strength emphasis
• TSA AUC: ALL = 0.604; SP = 0.704; BSP = 0.709; LM = 0.667 — position-normed TSA substantially better
• For every 1-point increase in 40-yd sprint z-score, probability of being drafted increased 1.7–2.4x depending on position
• Cohen's d for TSA: SP = 1.94, BSP = 2.42, LM = 3.03 — large effect sizes across all groups
limitations: Findings specific to NFL SC participants; may not generalize to athletes in other leagues or development pathways; position groupings (SP, BSP, LM) simplify nuanced positional differences; confounding factors such as training background, collegiate performance, and anthropometric data not included; QBs and specialists excluded; prediction of draft selection is only partially explained by physical metrics alone.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches should use position-normed z-scores (not raw scores or league-wide averages) when benchmarking athletes against combine standards. The 40-yard sprint is the most discriminating combine metric and should receive the greatest emphasis in speed development programs targeting NFL draft preparation. TSA provides a useful holistic athleticism index, but its predictive value is enhanced substantially when calculated within positional groups.
athleteDev: In American football, position-specific z-scores and TSA from the NFL SC are effective tools for distinguishing elite athletes from sub-elite in talent identification. For collegiate S&C staff, using combine-normed positional benchmarks allows coaches to identify specific physical deficits (e.g., a lineman weak in bench press z-score but strong in sprint) and tailor development programs accordingly. The large Cohen's d values (1.94–3.03) confirm that physical test performance is a major separating factor between drafted and undrafted players.
rtp: The NFL SC physical testing battery — including 40-yd sprint, vertical jump, broad jump, bench press, 3-cone drill, and shuttle run — provides position-normed benchmarks that can be adapted as RTP performance targets following injury. Achieving position-group z-score thresholds for speed and power tests (particularly 40-yd dash and broad jump, which showed the strongest discriminatory power) can serve as objective RTP milestones for high-level football players recovering from lower extremity injuries.

citation: Mohammad N, Ghali M, Rodriguez RJ, Israel H, Cannada LK. Analyzing Ankle Injuries in the NFL: A Video-Based Investigation of the Impact of Artificial Turf on Injury Mechanisms. J Orthop Exp Innov. 2026;7(1).
abstract: This retrospective video-based study examined 78 ankle and foot injuries sustained during the 2022–2023 NFL season to evaluate the relationship between playing surface type and injury mechanism. Injuries were classified as contact or non-contact and compared across three surface types: natural grass, monofilament/dual-fiber (MFDF) turf, and slit-film turf. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, slit-film turf exhibited the highest rate of contact injuries (85.7%) but the lowest rate of non-contact injuries (14.3%), while natural grass showed the highest proportion of non-contact injuries (46.4%). Statistically significant differences in injury mechanism across surface types were found (p=0.015).
tldr: Slit-film turf in the NFL does not increase non-contact ankle injuries as commonly assumed — it actually showed the lowest non-contact injury rate (14.3%) while natural grass had the highest (46.4%). The relationship between turf type and ankle injury mechanism is more complex than the prevailing narrative suggests, and direct contact appears to drive most slit-film injuries rather than surface traction.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports (2022–2023 season); 78 ankle/foot injuries identified in QBs, WRs, RBs, TEs, CBs, and safeties; game footage from NFL+ reviewed by two independent evaluators (E1, E2) to classify mechanism of injury (MOI: contact vs. non-contact); discrepancies resolved by a third evaluator (E3); surface types categorized as natural grass, MFDF turf, and slit-film turf; Mann-Whitney U test for ranked distribution comparison; Pearson chi-square for surface × MOI association; binary logistic regression; significance set at p<0.05.
findings:
• 78 ankle/foot injuries analyzed: 30.8% non-contact, 69.2% direct contact
• Slit-film turf: highest contact injury proportion (85.7%), lowest non-contact (14.3%)
• Natural grass: highest non-contact injury proportion (46.4%)
• MFDF turf: intermediate non-contact proportion (27.6%)
• Significant association between surface type and MOI: chi-square p=0.015 (grass vs. slit-film p=0.018)
• No significant difference between grass and MFDF turf (p=0.14) or MFDF vs. slit-film (p=0.262)
• Offensive skill positions (WR, RB, TE) accounted for 84.9% of offensive injuries
• Fourth quarter had the highest injury rate (41%), suggesting fatigue as a contributing factor
• Average injured player age: 25.8 years; no significant association between surface and weather conditions
limitations: Single-season cohort (2022–2023) from one league limits generalizability and trend confirmation; retrospective video review with human evaluators introduces classification bias; publicly available injury reports may underreport less severe injuries; no biomechanical testing, cleat type, field maintenance data, or medical records available; variability in NFL team injury reporting practices; indoor stadiums standardized to 73°F, limiting temperature analysis for indoor games; reinjury not captured due to documentation inconsistency.
practicalImplications: NFL teams and facility managers should not assume slit-film turf uniformly increases non-contact lower extremity injury risk — the actual relationship between surface type and ankle injury mechanism is surface-specific and complex. Injury prevention strategies for ankle injuries on slit-film surfaces should focus on contact mechanics and collision readiness rather than solely traction-related interventions. Fatigue management in the fourth quarter is an important modifiable factor across all surface types.
athleteDev: In American football, ankle and foot injuries are most common in offensive skill positions (WR, RB, TE at 84.9% of offensive injuries), making ankle stability and strength training a priority for these position groups. The surface-dependent variation in injury mechanism (contact vs. non-contact) suggests that position-specific ankle injury prevention programs should account for the primary playing surface used in the athlete's venue. Younger players (mean age 25.8) are at the greatest risk, reinforcing the importance of ankle prehabilitation in early career development.
rtp: The finding that slit-film turf predominantly produces contact-driven ankle injuries (vs. non-contact injuries on natural grass) has implications for RTP decision-making: the surface on which an athlete will return should inform the functional testing emphasis. For athletes returning to slit-film fields, RTP protocols should emphasize contact readiness and lateral stability under external perturbation rather than traction-based stress tests alone. The elevated fourth-quarter injury rate also underscores the need for fatigue-condition testing before full RTP clearance.
